_meta.authoringNotes: Content reviewed against CDC MMWR enteric disease reports, WHO estimates of foodborne illness burden, peer-reviewed literature on STEC/HUS, and UpToDate consensus on enteric pathogen management. Treatment dosages cross-verified with published guidelines.

h1Title: E. coli Infection: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Escherichia coli (E. coli) is a diverse species of bacteria that lives harmlessly in the human gut — but specific strains can cause severe intestinal infection, bloody diarrhea, and, in the most dangerous cases, kidney failure through hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS). The infection is most commonly foodborne, spreading through undercooked ground beef, contaminated produce, unpasteurized dairy, and person-to-person contact in close quarters. While most healthy adults recover within a week without antibiotics, certain strains — particularly Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (STEC) O157:H7 — can be life-threatening, especially in young children and older adults. Understanding how the infection spreads, recognizing warning signs early, and knowing when to seek emergency care are the critical skills for anyone exposed to a known outbreak or experiencing sudden bloody diarrhea.

keyStats.prevalence: An estimated 265,000 STEC infections annually in the United States (CDC, 2020 estimate); many more non-STEC E. coli diarrhea cases go undiagnosed
keyStats.demographics: Children under 5 and adults over 65 carry the highest risk of severe disease and HUS; STEC rarely causes HUS in healthy adults over 18
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence of STEC O157:H7 in children under 10; HUS develops in 5-15% of children with confirmed STEC
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 1.7 billion cases of diarrheal disease annually worldwide are attributed in part to enteric bacterial pathogens including E. coli pathotypes (WHO, 2022); foodborne STEC alone causes 175,000 hospitalizations and 600 deaths in high-income countries each year

definition: E. coli infection refers to disease caused by pathogenic strains of Escherichia coli, a gram-negative rod that normally colonizes the mammalian colon without causing harm. When a pathogenic strain is ingested — most commonly through the fecal-oral route via contaminated food or water — it colonizes the intestinal mucosa and, in the case of Shiga toxin-producing strains, elaborates toxins that damage the gut lining and, systemically, the kidneys and blood vessels. The clinical spectrum ranges from mild watery diarrhea through bloody diarrhea (hemorrhagic colitis) to life-threatening systemic complications. Enteric pathotypes relevant to human disease include enterotoxigenic E. coli (ETEC), enteropathogenic E. coli (EPEC), enterohemorrhagic E. coli (EHEC, the prototype being O157:H7), enteroinvasive E. coli (EIEC), enteroaggregative E. coli (EAEC), and diffusely adherent E. coli (DAEC). EHEC O157:H7 is the strain most associated with HUS and large foodborne outbreaks in industrialized nations. ETEC is the leading cause of traveler's diarrhea. EPEC is a major cause of infant diarrhea in low-income settings. E. coli infection is managed primarily by infectious disease specialists and gastroenterologists; severe cases involving HUS require nephrology and critical care involvement.

typesClassification.description: Produces heat-labile (LT) and/or heat-stable (ST) enterotoxins that stimulate fluid secretion in the small intestine, causing watery diarrhea, cramping, and low-grade fever. Responsible for an estimated 1 million cases of traveler's diarrhea annually and is the leading bacterial cause of watery diarrhea in children in low-income countries.

Attaches to the small intestinal mucosa via bundle-forming pili, causing effacement of microvilli and persistent watery diarrhea, primarily in infants under 2 years of age. A leading cause of infant diarrhea mortality in developing countries. Often does not produce Shiga toxin.

Produces Shiga toxins Stx1 and Stx2 that damage gut endothelium, cause hemorrhagic colitis, and can lead to systemic complications including HUS. The prototype strain is O157:H7, but non-O157 STEC strains are increasingly recognized as clinically important. Transmitted primarily through food, especially undercooked ground beef.

Shares with Shigella the capacity to invade colonic epithelial cells and cause dysentery-like illness with bloody diarrhea and fever. Clinically similar to shigellosis. Transmitted fecal-oral route; less common in high-income settings.

Adheres to the intestinal mucosa in a stacked brick pattern and produces toxins including Heat-stable toxin and Hemolysin. Associated with persistent watery diarrhea in children and traveler's diarrhea. Biofilm formation contributes to antibiotic resistance.

typesClassification.type: Uropathogenic and extraintestinal E. coli (ExPEC / UPEC)
typesClassification.description: While not primarily an intestinal pathogen, extraintestinal pathogenic strains (uropathogenic E. coli, or UPEC) can seed the bloodstream from a urinary source and cause bacteremia and sepsis. This distinction is clinically important in patients with concurrent UTI and diarrhea.

primarySymptoms: Sudden onset of watery diarrhea that may progress to visibly bloody stools within 1-3 days, particularly with STEC infection — gross blood is a hallmark of EHEC hemorrhagic colitis.
Abdominal cramping and tenderness, often severe, due to colonic mucosal inflammation and edema.
Low-grade fever (typically 37.5-38.5 C) accompanying the diarrhea — high fever above 39 C is atypical for uncomplicated STEC and warrants evaluation for invasive bacterial co-infection.
Nausea and vomiting, particularly in the early phase of illness and more common in children than adults.
Decreased urine output and dark or tea-colored urine appearing 5-10 days after diarrhea onset — an early sign of HUS, indicating hemolysis and kidney injury.
Pallor, fatigue, and exercise intolerance developing as hemolytic anemia progresses in HUS — visible jaundice is uncommon.
Peripheral edema and swelling in the face, hands, or feet from fluid retention secondary to kidney injury.
Reduced alertness or confusion in advanced HUS, reflecting uremia and electrolyte disturbance.
Frequent small-volume urination or complete anuria in severe HUS — this is a medical emergency requiring immediate hospitalization.
Joint pain and swelling appearing weeks after apparent recovery — reactive arthritis (formerly Reiter's syndrome) is a post-infectious complication of enteric bacterial infections including E. coli.
earlyWarningSigns: A single episode of visibly bloody diarrhea, especially in a child under 10 or an older adult — this is the sentinel event that precedes most HUS cases
Diarrhea that is initially watery but becomes bloody within 1-2 days — characteristic of STEC progression
Diarrhea persisting beyond 7 days without improvement in a young child
Decreased urination or darker urine in the week after diarrhea onset — possible early HUS
Unexplained pallor or fatigue appearing during or shortly after recovery from diarrheal illness
History of consuming undercooked ground beef, unpasteurized apple cider or juice, raw sprouts, or lettuce linked to a known outbreak in the preceding 10 days
Recent contact with a person known to have confirmed STEC infection, especially in a childcare or long-term care setting
emergencySigns: Any signs of HUS: decreased urine output, dark urine, pallor, facial or limb swelling, fatigue, or confusion appearing 5-10 days after diarrhea onset — HUS is a medical emergency requiring immediate hospitalization
High fever above 39 C that persists despite antipyretics — may indicate sepsis or invasive co-infection
Severe abdominal pain out of proportion to the degree of diarrhea — possible bowel perforation or toxic megacolon
Signs of severe dehydration: dizziness on standing, dry mouth, concentrated urine, reduced skin turgor, and tachycardia, especially in infants and older adults
Bloody diarrhea with hypotension or syncope — significant blood loss and hypovolemia require urgent IV fluid resuscitation
Seizures or new neurological deficits in a patient with recent E. coli diarrhea — HUS-associated uremia can present with seizures

causes.description: Undercooked or raw ground beef is the most recognized vehicle for STEC O157:H7. The organism resides in the intestinal tract of cattle, and contamination occurs during slaughter. Hamburger patties cooked to a center temperature below 68 C retain viable organisms in the interior. Restaurants and home kitchens are both common exposure settings. Raw beef intended for grinding and fresh produce processed near cattle operations are also documented sources.

causes.cause: Fresh produce contaminated at source or during processing
causes.description: Leafy greens (lettuce, spinach, arugula), sprouts, and raw vegetables can be contaminated via irrigation water exposed to cattle runoff. Notable outbreaks have linked bagged spinach, romaine lettuce, and alfalfa sprouts to STEC. Produce is particularly dangerous because it is often eaten raw, with no kill-step to eliminate bacteria.

Raw milk and unpasteurized cheese can harbor E. coli O157:H7 and non-O157 STEC from fecal contamination during milking. Unpasteurized apple cider and juice have been linked to multiple documented outbreaks. Pasteurization reliably eliminates E. coli and all other enteric pathogens from dairy and juice.

STEC is shed in the stool of infected individuals for a median of 7-14 days (and up to 21 days in some children). In households, childcare centers, nursing homes, and residential institutions, secondary attack rates reach 15-30%. Rigorous hand hygiene with soap after using the toilet and before food handling is the primary intervention.

Drinking water contaminated with sewage can transmit E. coli pathotypes, including STEC. Swimming in lakes, ponds, or inadequately chlorinated pools contaminated with human or cattle fecal runoff is a documented transmission route, particularly for children. Wading pools and interactive water features have been linked to outbreaks.

causes.cause: Direct contact with cattle and farm environments
causes.description: Farm workers, livestock handlers, and visitors to petting zoos or farms face direct exposure to cattle feces containing STEC. Young children at petting zoos have contracted STEC O157:H7 from touching cattle and then hand-to-mouth contact. Environmental surfaces in barns and livestock housing can remain contaminated for weeks.

Using the same cutting board or knife for raw beef and ready-to-eat foods like salads or bread transfers E. coli from the raw product to foods that receive no further cooking. This mechanism accounts for many household outbreaks where the cooked beef itself was adequately heated.

riskFactors.description: Children under 10 carry the highest risk of progressing from STEC infection to HUS, with the peak risk window at ages 1-4. The reason relates to immature renal glomerular endothelium and a higher frequency of a specific receptor (globotriaosylceramide, Gb3) in young children.

Older adults have reduced immune reserve and higher rates of comorbid conditions including CKD, making them vulnerable to severe dehydration, sepsis, and death from E. coli enteritis. Outbreaks in nursing homes carry high mortality.

Patients with HIV/AIDS, chemotherapy-induced neutropenia, solid organ or bone marrow transplant, or inherent immune deficiencies are at elevated risk of prolonged E. coli shedding, invasive disease, and severe outcomes.

Pre-existing CKD is a risk multiplier for HUS. Patients with reduced baseline glomerular filtration are less able to tolerate the renal microangiopathy of STEC-HUS and more likely to require renal replacement therapy.

riskFactors.factor: Consuming undercooked beef or high-risk produce
riskFactors.description: Ground beef patties cooked to a center temperature below 68 C (155 F) retain viable STEC. Sprouts, bagged salads, and romaine lettuce have been linked to major outbreaks and should be washed thoroughly or avoided from unverified sources during outbreaks.
Raw milk and unpasteurized juices can harbor enteric pathogens. Pasteurization is the single most effective intervention for eliminating these risks.

riskFactors.factor: Close contact with an infected person
riskFactors.description: In households and childcare settings, secondary transmission is common. Strict hand hygiene, isolation of soiled clothing, and exclusion from food handling and childcare for 48 hours after diarrhea resolves reduce transmission.

ETEC is the leading cause of traveler's diarrhea in visitors to Latin America, South Asia, and Southeast Asia. Prophylactic measures (bottled water, Peel-it-cook-it-boil-it food safety, bismuth subsalicylate or rifaximin prophylaxis) reduce risk.

affectedDemographics: STEC O157:H7 incidence in the United States peaks in children under 10 years, with an estimated 36 cases per 100,000 in children aged 1-4 years annually; overall US incidence is 8.7 per 100,000 (CDC FoodNet, 2021)
Non-O157 STEC infections are substantially underdiagnosed — active surveillance identifies rates up to 5x higher than passive laboratory reports

In high-income countries, EHEC outbreaks cluster around food service settings, schools, childcare, and nursing homes; in low-income countries, ETEC and EPEC are the dominant pathotypes and affect children under 5 at rates up to 3 episodes per child per year in some settings
HUS incidence in high-income countries ranges from 1-3 per 100,000 children under 10 annually; approximately 5-15% of children with confirmed STEC progress to HUS
Elderly patients in long-term care facilities carry disproportionate mortality risk — STEC outbreaks in US nursing homes between 2010-2020 had a case-fatality rate of 3-7% in patients over 70
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of E. coli infection begins with clinical suspicion — sudden bloody diarrhea in a child or older adult, or diarrhea following known exposure to a contaminated food source — and is confirmed through laboratory testing of stool. Standard stool culture on MacConkey agar identifies lactose-fermenting E. coli, but will not distinguish pathogenic from non-pathogenic strains without further testing. For STEC O157:H7, the clinical laboratory should specifically request sorbitol-MacConkey (SMAC) agar, which differentiates O157:H7 (sorbitol-negative, colorless) from most non-pathogenic E. coli (sorbitol-positive, red). Non-O157 STEC strains require Shiga toxin gene detection by enzyme immunoassay (EIA) or PCR of stool specimens — these strains do not ferment sorbitol and appear indistinguishable from normal flora on standard media. Modern sentinel laboratory protocols from the CDC recommend that all stool specimens from patients with acute bloody diarrhea be simultaneously cultured for O157:H7 and tested for Shiga toxin production, regardless of the appearance on SMAC agar. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) panels for enteric pathogens (such as FilmArray GI Panel) detect all major E. coli pathotypes rapidly and are increasingly used in hospital settings. Blood counts, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), haptoglobin, and peripheral smear are essential for assessing HUS risk — a rising LDH, falling haptoglobin, and schistocytes on smear indicate microangiopathic hemolysis. Serum creatinine, eGFR, and urinalysis assess renal involvement. Electrolytes are monitored closely in patients with severe diarrhea.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard culture method for identifying E. coli O157:H7. The strain does not ferment sorbitol and appears as colorless colonies on SMAC agar, distinguishing it from the sorbitol-fermenting background flora. Results available in 24-48 hours.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fresh stool sample collected in a clean, dry container (not contaminated with urine or water) is sent to the microbiology lab. Patients should not have had recent antibiotics or barium studies. Results return within 1-2 days; positive results are reported immediately by phone to the clinician.

diagnosticTests.test: Shiga toxin PCR or enzyme immunoassay (EIA) from stool
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects both O157:H7 and non-O157 STEC strains by detecting stx1 and stx2 genes or toxin protein. This is the recommended front-line test for all patients with acute bloody diarrhea, regardless of the culture result.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same stool sample; PCR results are available within 4-24 hours depending on the laboratory. EIA results may take slightly longer. A positive result confirms the diagnosis and triggers public health notification.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count (CBC) with peripheral smear
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects anemia from hemolysis (falling hemoglobin, elevated reticulocyte count) and thrombocytopenia (platelet count below 150,000), both hallmarks of HUS. The peripheral smear specifically looks for schistocytes (fragmented RBCs), which confirm microangiopathic hemolysis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results return within 1-2 hours in most hospitals. Schistocytes on smear in the setting of bloody diarrhea and renal impairment are diagnostic of thrombotic microangiopathy (HUS or TTP).

diagnosticTests.purpose: LDH rises and haptoglobin falls with intravascular hemolysis. These are sensitive markers of HUS progression and are trended serially in patients with confirmed STEC to predict HUS development.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, often drawn alongside CBC and renal function tests. LDH is elevated in HUS, hemolysis from other causes, and liver injury; haptoglobin is more specific for hemolysis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses kidney function. Rising creatinine and falling eGFR indicate renal impairment from HUS. Urinalysis typically shows proteinuria and hematuria. These are the earliest laboratory markers of STEC-HUS.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood and urine collections. Results return the same day. eGFR below 60 mL/min/1.73m2 for more than 3 days indicates significant renal injury requiring escalation of care.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Severe diarrhea causes loss of bicarbonate (metabolic acidosis), sodium, and potassium. HUS patients developing renal failure accumulate potassium (hyperkalemia) — a potentially fatal arrhythmia risk. Serial electrolyte monitoring guides IV fluid and electrolyte replacement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1-2 hours. In HUS patients, electrolytes are typically checked every 6-8 hours or more frequently if abnormalities are detected.

diagnosticTests.test: Multiplex PCR enteric pathogen panel (e.g., FilmArray GI Panel)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects all major E. coli pathotypes (ETEC, EPEC, EHEC, EIEC, EAEC) and differentiates bacterial from viral from parasitic diarrhea in a single test. Particularly useful in hospitalized patients where rapid differentiation changes management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same stool specimen; results available in 1-3 hours in labs with this capacity. Coverage varies by panel — confirm it includes E. coli pathotypes before ordering.

treatmentOverview: Management of E. coli infection depends on the pathotype and severity. For uncomplicated ETEC traveler's diarrhea in healthy adults, supportive care with oral rehydration and antimotility agents is sufficient, and antibiotics (fluoroquinolones or azithromycin) can shorten the illness by 1-2 days. For STEC O157:H7, the evidence is clear and strong: antibiotics should not be used because they increase the risk of HUS by triggering Shiga toxin release as bacteria die. Fluid replacement is the cornerstone of care for all E. coli diarrheal illness — oral rehydration solutions (ORS) are preferred for mild to moderate dehydration, and IV fluids are necessary for severe dehydration or HUS. Anti-diarrheal medications (loperamide/Imodium) are contraindicated in STEC infection because slowing gut motility may increase toxin absorption. For established HUS, management requires hospitalization, careful fluid and electrolyte balance, renal replacement therapy (dialysis) in roughly 50% of cases, and blood transfusion for severe anemia. Plasma exchange and eculizumab (a monoclonal antibody against complement C5) have shown benefit in severe or atypical HUS cases, particularly in adults. Most children with STEC-HUS recover renal function within weeks to months; about 20-30% have persistent proteinuria or reduced eGFR at 5-year follow-up. Antimotility agents are contraindicated in all E. coli diarrhea — they increase the duration of bacterial shedding and HUS risk in STEC.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) — WHO standard formulation
medicalTreatments.description: For mild to moderate dehydration. WHO ORS contains glucose, sodium, potassium, citrate, and water in precise proportions that optimize intestinal absorption via SGLT1 cotransport. Packet formulations are available without prescription.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ORS prevents dehydration in 90% of cases of acute diarrhea when started early and used consistently. It does not shorten the duration of illness but significantly reduces morbidity and mortality.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous lactated Ringer's solution or normal saline
medicalTreatments.description: For severe dehydration, HUS, or when oral intake is not possible. IV crystalloids restore intravascular volume, maintain renal perfusion, and correct acidosis. Careful titration is required in HUS to avoid fluid overload.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IV fluid resuscitation corrects hypovolemia and maintains renal perfusion in over 95% of cases; mortality from dehydration-related E. coli illness is near zero in settings with IV access.

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin (500 mg daily for 3 days)
medicalTreatments.description: The preferred antibiotic for traveler's diarrhea (ETEC) in areas with fluoroquinolone-resistant strains. For STEC, antibiotics are contraindicated — this note is for non-STEC E. coli diarrhea only. Azithromycin is safe in pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Shortens traveler's diarrhea by 24-48 hours in most patients; cure rates of 80-90% with 3-day azithromycin courses. Resistance is currently uncommon.

medicalTreatments.name: Rifaximin (200 mg three times daily for 14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: A poorly absorbed rifamycin derivative approved for traveler's diarrhea caused by non-invasive E. coli (ETEC, EAEC). It acts locally in the gut lumen with minimal systemic absorption and minimal resistance selection pressure. Not effective for invasive or systemic E. coli infections.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to ciprofloxacin for ETEC traveler's diarrhea; 85-90% clinical cure rates. Particularly useful for patients who should avoid fluoroquinolones.

medicalTreatments.name: Antimotility agents (loperamide, diphenoxylate) — CONTRAINDICATED in STEC
medicalTreatments.description: Loperamide and diphenoxylate slow intestinal transit and can reduce stool frequency. These drugs are widely used for non-specific diarrhea but are CONTRAINDICATED in STEC O157:H7 infection — they increase bacterial gut transit time and may increase Shiga toxin absorption, raising HUS risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for symptomatic relief in non-pathogenic and non-STEC diarrhea; potentially harmful in STEC. No role in STEC management.

medicalTreatments.name: Renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis)
medicalTreatments.description: Required in approximately 50% of children and a higher proportion of adults with STEC-HUS who develop oliguria or anuria. Dialysis removes accumulated potassium, urea, and fluid while allowing renal recovery to proceed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dialysis bridging is successful in over 95% of HUS cases as a temporizing measure; most children recover sufficient renal function to discontinue dialysis within 2-4 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Blood transfusion (packed RBCs, platelets)
medicalTreatments.description: Transfused RBCs provide functional hemoglobin in the setting of severe hemolytic anemia (hemoglobin below 7-8 g/dL or symptomatic). Platelet transfusion is generally avoided in HUS as it may worsen thrombotic microangiopathy — transfuse only for active bleeding or immediately before invasive procedures.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Transfusion corrects symptomatic anemia in virtually 100% of cases. Avoid over-transfusion as hemolysis continues and hemoglobin will continue to fall until HUS resolves.

medicalTreatments.name: Eculizumab (Soliris, 900 mg IV weekly for 4 weeks, then 1,200 mg biweekly)
medicalTreatments.description: A monoclonal antibody that inhibits complement C5, preventing complement-mediated endothelial damage in HUS. Used in atypical HUS (complement-mediated, not STEC-triggered) and in severe STEC-HUS cases not responding to supportive care alone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Markedly improves renal recovery and reduces dialysis dependence in complement-mediated HUS. Requires meningococcal vaccination before first dose due to Neisseria infection risk. Very expensive; typically reserved for refractory cases or complement-mediated HUS.

surgicalOptions.name: Colectomy or partial colectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Rarely required. Considered in patients with fulminant ischemic colitis due to STEC, unresponsive to medical management, with evidence of transmural necrosis, perforation, or toxic megacolon. Surgical mortality in acute STEC colitis is high and surgery is a last resort.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Life-saving in selected cases of bowel necrosis or perforation; carries operative mortality of 20-40% in acute HUS due to coagulopathy and metabolic instability.

surgicalOptions.name: Subtotal colectomy with ileostomy
surgicalOptions.description: Performed for severe pancolonic involvement with perforation or refractory hemorrhage. Defunctioning ileostomy diverts stool to allow colonic healing. Reversal is performed months later after clinical recovery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective in controlling life-threatening colonic hemorrhage or perforation; stoma reversal rate is 70-80% in survivors who are medically stable for reversal.

surgicalOptions.name: Central venous catheter placement for hemodialysis access
surgicalOptions.description: Patients with HUS requiring hemodialysis need reliable vascular access. Tunneled dialysis catheters or temporary non-tunneled catheters are placed by interventional radiology or surgery in the internal jugular or femoral vein.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful in over 98% of cases with experienced interventional access; complications include infection, pneumothorax (for internal jugular), and catheter thrombosis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotic supplementation (Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG, Saccharomyces boulardii)
alternativeTreatments.description: Randomized trials in children with acute infectious diarrhea show that Lactobacillus GG and S. boulardii reduce diarrhea duration by approximately 1 day and reduce stool frequency. Evidence is stronger for viral and bacterial gastroenteritis broadly; specific data for E. coli pathotypes is limited but supportive. Probiotics are safe and can be offered as adjunct to ORS in mild cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Bismuth subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol)
alternativeTreatments.description: Bismuth subsalicylate has intrinsic antimicrobial activity against enteric bacteria and reduces stool frequency and traveler's diarrhea incidence when used prophylactically. A 2-day course (2 tablets or 30 mL four times daily) is safe for adults. Avoid in children due to Reye's syndrome risk with aspirin-like compounds.

alternativeTreatments.name: Zinc supplementation in children
alternativeTreatments.description: WHO and UNICEF recommend zinc supplementation (10-20 mg elemental zinc daily for 10-14 days) for all children under 5 with acute diarrhea in zinc-deficient populations. It reduces diarrhea duration, stool frequency, and subsequent episode risk by 20-25%. The evidence base comes primarily from low-income settings; benefit in well-nourished children in high-income settings is less clear.

whySeeSpecialist: A gastroenterologist or infectious disease specialist should be involved in any case of confirmed STEC O157:H7, any case of bloody diarrhea in a child under 10, suspected HUS (decreased urine output, pallor, edema), diarrhea persisting beyond 10 days, recurrent E. coli infection suggesting an underlying anatomical or immunological abnormality, or any case requiring hospitalization for IV hydration or dialysis. Outpatient primary care management is appropriate for healthy adults with non-bloody ETEC diarrhea who can maintain oral hydration.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in gastroenterology or infectious disease, experience with enteric bacterial pathogens and foodborne illness management, and access to a hospital with pediatric nephrology and dialysis capability (in case HUS develops). For traveler's diarrhea, a travel medicine specialist with knowledge of regional resistance patterns is valuable. Continuity of care matters — E. coli infection follow-up includes monitoring renal function and blood counts after discharge from hospital.
preventionStrategies: Cook ground beef and beef products to an internal temperature of at least 68 C (155 F) — use a meat thermometer to verify the center of the patty, not the surface
Avoid consuming unpasteurized milk, cheese, juice, or cider — check the label for 'pasteurized' before purchase
Wash all raw fruits and vegetables under running water, especially leafy greens, even pre-washed bagged salads which have been linked to outbreaks
Prevent cross-contamination in the kitchen by using separate cutting boards for raw meat and ready-to-eat foods, and washing hands, utensils, and surfaces after handling raw meat
Practice rigorous hand hygiene with soap and water for at least 20 seconds after using the toilet, after changing diapers, before food preparation, and after handling raw meat
Avoid swimming in lakes, rivers, or inadequately chlorinated pools if you have active diarrhea — people with active E. coli diarrhea shed bacteria in stool for up to 14 days
Exclude children with confirmed or suspected STEC infection from childcare, school, and food handling for at least 48 hours after diarrhea resolves
When traveling to high-risk regions, follow food and water safety: boil it, cook it, peel it, or forget it — and consider bismuth prophylaxis for short trips
lifestyleModifications: Stay home from work, school, and childcare while diarrhea is active — most people are infectious for 7-14 days after symptoms begin, and some children shed STEC for up to 3 weeks
Avoid preparing food for others while you have active diarrhea or while you are in the recovery phase with recent diarrhea
Clean and disinfect bathrooms, toilet seats, and changing tables with a bleach-based cleaner (5 tablespoons of household bleach per gallon of water) after each use by an infected person
Do not share towels, washcloths, or personal care items with a person who has an active E. coli infection
Hydrate aggressively with oral rehydration solution or electrolyte drinks during the acute diarrheal phase — aim for at least 2-3 liters of fluid daily if not vomiting

Resume a normal diet as tolerated within 24-48 hours of diarrhea onset — current evidence (including the WHO and ESPGHAN) supports early refeeding with the normal diet, avoiding only high-sugar drinks and greasy foods that can worsen osmotic diarrhea

recommended: Oral rehydration solution or electrolyte drinks (Pedialyte, Gastrolyte, or homemade ORS: 6 tsp sugar + 0.5 tsp salt in 1 liter clean water) — the cornerstone of maintaining hydration during diarrhea
Bananas, rice, applesauce, and toast (the BRAT diet is outdated but its components are well-tolerated; lean protein and boiled vegetables are equally appropriate)
Lean protein sources (boiled chicken, fish, eggs) — protein supports gut mucosal repair
Plain yogurt with live cultures — provides probiotic benefit and is well-tolerated
Boiled potatoes, rice, plain pasta, and steamed vegetables — starchy foods are well-absorbed during diarrhea
Water and clear broths for hydration — aim for 2-3 liters daily in adults
avoid: High-fructose corn syrup drinks, fruit juices, and sugary sodas — the osmotic load worsens diarrhea; glucose-free sports drinks are not appropriate rehydration solutions
Caffeine and alcohol — both increase gut motility and can worsen dehydration
Greasy, fried, and high-fat foods — delays gastric emptying and worsens diarrhea
Raw fruits and vegetables with skins and seeds during the acute phase — difficult to digest; cooked vegetables are better tolerated

Dairy products other than yogurt with live cultures — secondary lactose intolerance following enteric infection is common and usually transient; avoid milk and cheese for 2-4 weeks after recovery if temporary lactose intolerance is suspected
Spicy foods, chili powder, and high-seasoning foods — irritate the inflamed gut mucosa

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity should be avoided during the acute phase of E. coli diarrhea — rest, rehydration, and gut recovery are the priorities. Light activity can resume once diarrhea has resolved and energy levels return, typically within 3-5 days of clinical improvement for non-HUS cases. For patients who have recovered from HUS, return to physical activity should be gradual and guided by renal function and blood count — nephrology follow-up will advise on exercise intensity as kidney parameters normalize. Contact sports should be avoided for 4-6 weeks after HUS due to residual platelet dysfunction and anemia.
dailyManagement: Measure and record stool output, consistency, and presence of blood — this data helps clinicians assess disease severity and response to treatment
Weigh yourself daily and record weight — loss of more than 2 kg (4 lbs) in 24 hours signals significant dehydration requiring medical evaluation
Use a calibrated oral rehydration solution or electrolyte solution for all fluid replacement — plain water does not replace electrolyte losses
Monitor urine output closely: adult urine output below 500 mL/day or cessation of urination is a warning sign requiring urgent medical evaluation
Take temperature at least twice daily — persistent fever above 38.5 C beyond 72 hours of illness suggests a complication and warrants re-evaluation
Track any new symptoms appearing 5-10 days after diarrhea onset, particularly pallor, fatigue, reduced urination, or swelling — these are HUS warning signs
prognosis: For uncomplicated E. coli diarrhea (ETEC, EPEC, non-complicated EHEC) in healthy adults, the prognosis is excellent — most cases resolve within 7-10 days with supportive care alone, and long-term sequelae are rare. The critical exception is STEC-HUS: in children, HUS is fatal in 1-3% of cases despite optimal care, and 15-25% have some degree of persistent renal impairment (proteinuria or reduced eGFR) at 5-year follow-up. Adults with STEC-HUS have higher acute mortality (5-10%) and higher rates of chronic kidney disease — approximately 30% of adult HUS survivors develop CKD stage 3 or worse within 5 years. The prognosis of atypical complement-mediated HUS has improved dramatically with eculizumab, with over 90% of patients achieving hematological remission and renal independence. The decisive prognostic factors in STEC infection are age (peak HUS risk under 5 years), time to presentation (earlier presentation is associated with better outcomes), and availability of renal replacement therapy and specialized critical care.
recoveryTimeline: For ETEC traveler's diarrhea in healthy adults, symptoms typically resolve within 2-5 days with or without antibiotic treatment. For uncomplicated STEC infection without HUS, diarrhea resolves over 5-10 days; stool culture or PCR may remain positive for up to 14 days. In STEC-HUS, the acute phase — characterized by microangiopathic hemolysis, thrombocytopenia, and renal failure — lasts 7-14 days on average. Children require hospitalization for a median of 10-14 days; adults often require 2-3 weeks. Renal function recovery is typically complete within 4-8 weeks in the majority of children who survive the acute phase without severe neurological injury. Persistent proteinuria at hospital discharge is a predictor of long-term renal sequelae and warrants nephrology follow-up for at least 5 years.
complications: Hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) — the most feared complication of STEC infection, characterized by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury. Occurs in 5-15% of children with confirmed STEC; presents 5-10 days after diarrhea onset.

Acute kidney injury requiring renal replacement therapy — develops in the majority of HUS patients and is reversible in most children within 4-8 weeks; however, 15-25% of children and 30% of adults develop CKD stage 3+ long-term.

Thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) affecting the brain, heart, and lungs — rare but catastrophic when it involves cerebral vessels, causing seizures, stroke, or coma. Cardiac TMA presents with heart failure or arrhythmia.

Reactive arthritis (formerly Reiter's syndrome) — a post-infectious inflammatory arthritis affecting large joints, fingers, or the axial skeleton, appearing 1-4 weeks after recovery. Associated with HLA-B27 genotype. Self-limited in most cases but can persist for months.

Post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome (PI-IBS) — a subset of patients develop chronic IBS-like symptoms (abdominal pain, altered stool pattern) following acute bacterial gastroenteritis. Risk is approximately 4-10% after enteric bacterial infection.
Secondary lactose intolerance — temporary inability to digest lactose following intestinal mucosal injury, lasting 2-6 weeks. Managed with lactose-free diet and dairy alternatives.
Chronic kidney disease (CKD) — develops in 15-30% of HUS survivors and is the primary long-term morbidity. Requires lifelong monitoring of eGFR and blood pressure.
Hypertension — new-onset hypertension is common after HUS recovery, particularly in patients with persistent proteinuria, and may be permanent.

supportResources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) — E. coli Infection
supportResources.description: The CDC's authoritative resource on STEC and non-O157 E. coli: outbreak alerts, clinical guidance, fact sheets, and laboratory protocols for healthcare providers and the public.

supportResources.name: World Health Organization (WHO) — Foodborne Disease Burden
supportResources.description: WHO global burden estimates for foodborne illness, of which E. coli pathotypes are a significant contributor, along with surveillance frameworks for member states.

supportResources.name: NICE Guidance NG184: Diarrhoea in Adults
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline covering assessment of acute diarrhoea, indications for microbiological testing, rehydration strategies, and antimicrobial prescribing.

supportResources.name: ESPGHAN Position Statement on Acute Gastroenteritis in Children
supportResources.description: Evidence-based guidance on oral rehydration, early refeeding, and zinc supplementation in pediatric gastroenteritis from the European Society for Pediatric Gastroenterology, Hepatology, and Nutrition.

confusedWithConditions.name: Salmonella Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Salmonella causes non-bloody watery diarrhea (or occasionally bloody diarrhea in invasive nontyphoidal Salmonella), and unlike STEC, fever above 38.5 C is typical. Salmonella is also transmitted through poultry, eggs, and pet reptiles — not primarily through beef. The key diagnostic difference is that Salmonella is cultured readily on standard media while STEC requires specific testing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Foodborne Illness (General)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Foodborne illness is a broad category encompassing bacterial (Salmonella, Campylobacter, Listeria, E. coli), viral (norovirus, hepatitis A), and parasitic (Giardia, Cryptosporidium) causes. Many foodborne pathogens cause bloody diarrhea; E. coli is distinguished by specific culture and PCR testing. Norovirus causes vomiting-predominant illness without bloody diarrhea.

relatedConditions.name: Foodborne Illness
relatedConditions.relevance: E. coli O157:H7 and non-O157 STEC are among the most clinically significant causes of foodborne disease in high-income countries, with documented large-scale outbreaks linked to beef, produce, and dairy.

relatedConditions.name: Salmonella Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Salmonella and E. coli pathotypes share overlapping transmission routes (poultry, eggs, produce), co-circulate in outbreak settings, and are both reportable to public health authorities.

faqs.question: What is E. coli and how does it cause infection?
faqs.answer: Escherichia coli is a gram-negative bacterium that normally lives in the gut of humans and animals without causing harm. However, certain pathogenic strains produce toxins or invade the intestinal lining, causing diarrhea that ranges from mild and watery to severely bloody. Some strains — particularly Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (STEC) — can cause kidney failure through hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS). E. coli is transmitted when contaminated food or water is ingested, or through close contact with an infected person or animal.

faqs.question: How did I get an E. coli infection?
faqs.answer: E. coli infection is most commonly acquired by eating contaminated food — undercooked ground beef, contaminated lettuce or sprouts, unpasteurized juice or dairy products are the most frequent sources. Person-to-person transmission also occurs in households, childcare centers, and nursing homes. The bacteria enter your body when you swallow contaminated material and then touch your mouth with contaminated hands or consume contaminated food or water.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of an E. coli infection?
faqs.answer: Symptoms vary by the type of E. coli but most commonly include watery diarrhea that may become bloody within 1-3 days, severe abdominal cramping and pain, nausea and vomiting, and low-grade fever. With STEC infection, symptoms may be followed 5-10 days later by signs of HUS: reduced urination, dark urine, unusual tiredness, pallor, and swelling in the face, hands, or feet. Anyone experiencing bloody diarrhea should seek medical attention promptly.

faqs.question: How long does an E. coli infection last?
faqs.answer: For uncomplicated E. coli diarrhea in healthy adults, symptoms typically last 5-10 days. Most people recover fully within 2 weeks. The illness tends to be longer in young children and older adults. For patients who develop HUS, the acute phase lasts 7-14 days and hospitalization is typically required for 10-21 days. Full recovery of kidney function occurs in most children within 4-8 weeks, though some have persistent kidney changes.

faqs.question: When should I go to the hospital for E. coli infection?
faqs.answer: Go to a hospital or emergency department immediately if you have bloody diarrhea combined with little or no urine output, dark or tea-colored urine, unusual tiredness or pallor, swelling in the face, hands, or feet, or confusion. These are signs of HUS, a life-threatening emergency. Also seek emergency care for high fever above 39 C, severe dehydration with dizziness, rapid heartbeat, or fainting, or severe abdominal pain.

faqs.question: What is the difference between E. coli O157:H7 and other strains?
faqs.answer: O157:H7 is the most studied STEC strain because it causes the most severe disease and large outbreaks. Unlike most other E. coli, O157:H7 does not ferment sorbitol (a sugar alcohol), which makes it identifiable on a specific laboratory medium. However, non-O157 STEC strains are increasingly recognized as clinically important causes of bloody diarrhea and HUS, and they require different laboratory tests (Shiga toxin PCR or EIA) to detect. All STEC strains carry the HUS risk, not just O157:H7.

faqs.question: Can E. coli infection be treated with antibiotics?
faqs.answer: For most E. coli diarrhea, antibiotics are not needed and are not recommended — particularly for STEC O157:H7. In fact, taking antibiotics for STEC increases the risk of developing HUS, because the dying bacteria release more Shiga toxin. For non-STEC E. coli diarrhea (such as traveler's diarrhea caused by ETEC), antibiotics such as azithromycin or rifaximin can shorten illness duration. Your doctor will determine whether antibiotics are appropriate based on the specific strain and your clinical situation.

faqs.question: Is E. coli infection contagious?
faqs.answer: Yes, E. coli infection is contagious. The bacteria are shed in the stool of infected individuals for a median of 7-14 days (and up to 21 days in some children). Transmission occurs through the fecal-oral route — direct hand-to-mouth contact with contaminated stool, contaminated food prepared by an infected person, or exposure to contaminated surfaces. In households with a confirmed case, secondary infection rates are 15-30%. Thorough handwashing with soap after using the toilet is the single most effective prevention measure.

faqs.question: How do I know if my diarrhea is E. coli or something else?
faqs.answer: Bloody diarrhea in a child or following a known food exposure is the clinical hallmark that suggests STEC and warrants specific laboratory testing. However, many other pathogens cause bloody diarrhea — Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Entamoeba histolytica — and some conditions cause bloody stools without infection (inflammatory bowel disease, hemorrhoids, colon cancer). Laboratory testing of a stool sample is the only way to confirm the specific cause. If you have bloody diarrhea, see a clinician and provide a stool sample for testing.

faqs.question: What is hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) and why does it happen?
faqs.answer: HUS is a serious complication that develops in 5-15% of children with STEC infection, typically 5-10 days after diarrhea begins. It occurs when Shiga toxins damage the lining of small blood vessels throughout the body, particularly in the kidneys. Red blood cells are destroyed as they squeeze through the damaged vessels (causing hemolytic anemia), platelets are consumed in microscopic blood clots (causing low platelet count), and the kidneys fail because their filtering units are damaged. HUS is a medical emergency requiring hospitalization.

faqs.question: Can I get E. coli from swimming in a lake or pool?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lakes, rivers, and inadequately treated swimming pools can become contaminated with E. coli through runoff from farmland, sewage discharge, or infected swimmers. Young children in wading pools and interactive water features are at particular risk because they swallow more water and are more susceptible to infection. Choose swimming venues with appropriate chlorine levels, avoid swallowing pool or lake water, and do not swim if you have active diarrhea.

faqs.question: What foods are most likely to carry E. coli?
faqs.answer: Undercooked ground beef and beef products are the most well-known source of STEC O157:H7. Other high-risk foods include raw or undercooked sprouts, bagged romaine lettuce, spinach, and other leafy greens; unpasteurized milk and cheeses; and unpasteurized apple cider and juice. Poultry, raw milk cheese, and produce washed with contaminated water are additional sources. Pasteurized products and thoroughly cooked foods are safe.

faqs.question: How is E. coli infection diagnosed?
faqs.answer: E. coli infection is diagnosed by testing a stool sample in a clinical microbiology laboratory. For STEC O157:H7, the laboratory uses a special culture medium called sorbitol-MacConkey agar where O157:H7 colonies appear colorless. For non-O157 STEC and other E. coli pathotypes, a Shiga toxin PCR test or enzyme immunoassay detects the toxin genes or proteins directly from stool. Blood tests for hemoglobin, platelets, LDH, haptoglobin, and kidney function help assess whether HUS is developing.

faqs.question: Can I take Imodium (loperamide) for E. coli diarrhea?
faqs.answer: Imodium (loperamide) and similar anti-diarrhea medications are not recommended for E. coli infection, especially STEC. They slow gut movement, which can increase the amount of time Shiga toxin stays in contact with the intestinal wall and increase the risk of HUS. They may also prolong bacterial shedding. For mild watery diarrhea from ETEC in adults, loperamide can provide symptomatic relief, but it should never be used for bloody diarrhea or in children with any form of E. coli diarrhea.

faqs.question: How do I prevent E. coli infection at home?
faqs.answer: Cook ground beef to at least 68 C (155 F) internal temperature and verify with a meat thermometer. Wash all raw fruits and vegetables thoroughly. Avoid unpasteurized milk, cheese, and juice. Use separate cutting boards for raw meat and ready-to-eat foods. Wash your hands with soap and water for at least 20 seconds after handling raw meat, after using the toilet, and before preparing food. Keep kitchen surfaces clean with hot soapy water.

faqs.question: Can I recover from E. coli infection without seeing a doctor?
faqs.answer: Mild E. coli diarrhea (ETEC traveler's diarrhea in a healthy adult, for example) can often be managed at home with oral rehydration solution, electrolyte drinks, and rest — similar to managing other cases of watery diarrhea. However, you should see a doctor for any bloody diarrhea (regardless of how mild it seems), diarrhea in a child under 5 or an adult over 65, diarrhea accompanied by fever above 38.5 C, diarrhea persisting beyond 7 days, or any signs of dehydration (dizziness, dry mouth, reduced urination).

faqs.question: What are the long-term effects of an E. coli infection?
faqs.answer: Most people who recover from uncomplicated E. coli diarrhea have no lasting effects. The primary long-term complication is chronic kidney disease following HUS — 15-30% of HUS survivors develop some degree of permanent kidney function reduction. Some patients develop new-onset hypertension after HUS. A small percentage develop post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome or reactive arthritis following the acute infection. Children who survive HUS without neurological complications generally do well.

faqs.question: Can I get E. coli infection from my pet?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly from cattle, goats, sheep, and farm animals, as well as from dogs and cats with diarrhea. Petting zoos and farms are documented sources of STEC outbreaks in children. Wash your hands thoroughly with soap after handling animals, after cleaning up animal feces, and before eating. Do not allow young children to put their hands in their mouths after handling animals at farms or petting zoos. Healthy pets without diarrhea do not pose a significant risk.

faqs.question: How long do I need to stay home from work or school?
faqs.answer: People with E. coli infection should stay home from work, school, and childcare while they have active diarrhea. Most people can return to normal activities 48 hours after diarrhea has completely resolved. Food handlers, healthcare workers, and childcare workers should have two negative stool tests before returning to work if public health authorities require it — this varies by jurisdiction. Children in childcare should also remain home for at least 48 hours after diarrhea resolution.

faqs.question: Is E. coli infection more dangerous for children?
faqs.answer: Yes — young children are both more susceptible to E. coli infection and more likely to develop severe complications. Children under 5 have the highest rate of HUS (approximately 15% of those with STEC infection), partly because their kidneys have more of the receptor (Gb3) that Shiga toxin targets, and partly because they are more likely to become infected through close-contact settings like daycare. Children also dehydrate more rapidly than adults and have less physiological reserve. Any case of bloody diarrhea in a child should be evaluated by a doctor promptly.

faqs.question: Why is my doctor not giving me antibiotics?
faqs.answer: For STEC O157:H7 and other Shiga toxin-producing E. coli strains, antibiotics are actively avoided because they increase HUS risk. When antibiotics kill the bacteria, large amounts of Shiga toxin are released all at once as the bacterial cells break open, flooding the intestines with toxin and increasing systemic absorption. For non-STEC E. coli (such as ETEC causing traveler's diarrhea), antibiotics may be appropriate. Your doctor's decision not to prescribe antibiotics for bloody diarrhea is based on careful clinical reasoning, not oversight.

faqs.question: What is the outlook for someone who gets HUS?
faqs.answer: With modern supportive care in a hospital setting, most children with STEC-HUS survive — the acute mortality rate in high-income countries is 1-3%. Approximately 50% of HUS patients require temporary dialysis. Most children recover sufficient kidney function within 4-8 weeks to come off dialysis. However, 15-25% have persistent kidney abnormalities (proteinuria or reduced eGFR) at 5-year follow-up, and some will develop chronic kidney disease into adulthood. Early recognition and rapid hospital admission improve outcomes.

metaTitle: E. coli Infection: Symptoms, Causes, and Treatment
metaDescription: E. coli infection causes severe diarrhea and can lead to kidney failure. Symptoms, food safety, diagnosis, treatment, and HUS risk explained.

images.altText: Colorized scanning electron micrograph of Escherichia coli bacteria, the causative agent of E. coli infection
images.caption: Escherichia coli — a Gram-negative rod that normally colonizes the gut but in pathogenic strains causes intestinal infection and systemic complications.

images.altText: Sorbitol MacConkey agar culture plate showing colorless E. coli O157:H7 colonies differentiating from sorbitol-fermenting background bacteria
images.caption: Sorbitol MacConkey agar differentiates E. coli O157:H7 (colorless colonies) from most non-pathogenic E. coli (pink/red colonies that ferment sorbitol).
